title: The Parallelization Ceiling

content.statement: Your total speedup is capped by the part of the work you can never parallelize.
content.explainer: No matter how many people or machines you throw at a task, the portion that must run in sequence sets a hard ceiling on how fast the whole thing can go. Beyond a point, adding more workers buys you almost nothing.
questions: What fraction of your core process genuinely cannot be split up and run at the same time?
Where are you adding people or tools to a workflow whose bottleneck is a step that can only run in sequence?
If you had unlimited parallel capacity tomorrow, what is the fastest this process could ever theoretically run?
sop: Break the process into steps that can run in parallel versus steps that must run in sequence.
Estimate the serial fraction, the share of total time stuck in sequential steps (see calculation, page 6).
Compute your realistic maximum speedup before you buy more capacity.
Attack the serial fraction directly by removing, automating, or redesigning those steps.
Add parallel resources only up to the point where the serial ceiling makes more pointless.

definitions.def: The share of a task that must be done one step at a time, in order, and cannot be sped up by adding more workers.
The share of a task that can be split into independent pieces and worked on simultaneously.
The number of parallel workers, processors, or resources you apply to the parallelizable part of the work.

content.equation: Max Speedup = 1 ÷ (Serial Fraction + Parallel Fraction ÷ N)
content.equation_example: Serial fraction 0.2, parallel fraction 0.8, and 4 parallel workers: 0.8 ÷ 4 = 0.2, then 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4, and 1 ÷ 0.4 = 2.5. Four workers give only a 2.5× speedup, and even infinite workers would cap at 1 ÷ 0.2 = 5×.
content.closing_statement: Real leverage comes from redesigning the bottleneck, not from piling more effort onto a limit that will never move.
content.question: What fraction of your core process genuinely cannot be split up and run at the same time?

caption:
Principle Nº 356: The Parallelization Ceiling

Your total speedup is capped by the part of the work you can never parallelize.

Ask yourself: What fraction of your core process genuinely cannot be split up and run at the same time?
